texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: EXPONENTIAL UTILITY MAXIMIZATION IN A DISCRETE TIME GAUSSIAN FRAMEWORK
texts.text: EXPONENTIAL UTILITY MAXIMIZATION IN A DISCRETE TIME GAUSSIAN FRAMEWORK

texts.label: text
texts.orig: YAN DOLINSKY AND OR ZUK
texts.text: YAN DOLINSKY AND OR ZUK

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. The aim of this short note is to present a solution to the discrete time exponential utility maximization problem in a case where the underlying asset has a multivariate normal distribution. In addition to the usual setting considered in Mathematical Finance, we also consider an investor who is informed about the risky asset's price changes with a delay D . Our method of solution is based on the theory developed in [4] and guessing the optimal portfolio.
texts.text: Abstract. The aim of this short note is to present a solution to the discrete time exponential utility maximization problem in a case where the underlying asset has a multivariate normal distribution. In addition to the usual setting considered in Mathematical Finance, we also consider an investor who is informed about the risky asset's price changes with a delay D . Our method of solution is based on the theory developed in [4] and guessing the optimal portfolio.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction and the Main Result
texts.text: 1. Introduction and the Main Result

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taking into account frictions is an important challenge in financial modelling. In this note, we focus on the friction arising from the fact that investment decisions may be based only on delayed information, and the actual present market price is unknown at the time of decision making. This corresponds to the case where there is a time delay in receiving market information (or in applying it), which causes the trader's filtration to be delayed with respect to the price filtration.
texts.text: Taking into account frictions is an important challenge in financial modelling. In this note, we focus on the friction arising from the fact that investment decisions may be based only on delayed information, and the actual present market price is unknown at the time of decision making. This corresponds to the case where there is a time delay in receiving market information (or in applying it), which causes the trader's filtration to be delayed with respect to the price filtration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start by briefly reviewing some of the relevant literature. In [18] the author solved the mean-variance hedging problem with partial information for European contingent claims in a setup where the asset price process is a martingale. Subsequently, the study of the mean-variance hedging problem was extended beyond the martingale setup (see for instance [7, 13]). The work [15] applied Malliavin calculus to the utility maximization problem, and in [17] the authors introduce and study a general framework of optimal stochastic control with delayed information. Another related work is [2] which deals with scaling limits of utility indifference prices with vanishing delay and risk aversion inversely proportional to the delay.
texts.text: We start by briefly reviewing some of the relevant literature. In [18] the author solved the mean-variance hedging problem with partial information for European contingent claims in a setup where the asset price process is a martingale. Subsequently, the study of the mean-variance hedging problem was extended beyond the martingale setup (see for instance [7, 13]). The work [15] applied Malliavin calculus to the utility maximization problem, and in [17] the authors introduce and study a general framework of optimal stochastic control with delayed information. Another related work is [2] which deals with scaling limits of utility indifference prices with vanishing delay and risk aversion inversely proportional to the delay.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To formulate our main result, let (Ω , F , P ) be a complete probability space carrying one risky asset which we denote by S = ( S k ) 0 ≤ k ≤ n where n ∈ N is a fixed finite time horizon. We assume that the investor has a bank account that, for simplicity, bears no interest. We assume that the initial stock price S 0 is a known constant and the random vector ( S 1 , ..., S n ) has a non-degenerate multivariate normal distribution. Hence,

texts.orig: We fix a nonnegative integer number D ∈ Z + and consider a situation where there is a delay of D trading times. Hence, the investor's flow of information is given by the filtration G D k := σ { S 0 , ..., S ( k -D ) + } , k = 0 , 1 , ..., n . The case D = 0 means that there is no delay and corresponds to the usual setting.

The case D = 0 means that there is no delay and corresponds to the usual setting.

texts.orig: Proof. The proof will be done in three steps.
texts.text: Proof. The proof will be done in three steps.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step I: In this step we provide an existence and uniqueness for the decomposition (1.2). We apply the results from [4] which perfectly fit our purposes. First we introduce some notations. Let C ∈ M n ( R ) and I, J ∈ { 1 , .., n } k be two integer vectors of length k ≤ n denoting row and column indices, satisfying I = ( i 1 , .., i k ) , J = ( j 1 , .., j k ) with 1 ≤ i 1 < i 2 < .. < i k ≤ n , 1 ≤ j 1 < j 2 < .. < j k ≤ n . We denote by C I J the minor specified by these indices, i.e. the determinant of the k × k submatrix determined by taking the elements C ij for i = i 1 , ..i k ; j = j 1 , ..j k . For any two natural numbers b ≥ a we denote the vector ( a, a +1 , ..., b ) by [ a : b ].

We denote by C I J the minor specified by these indices, i.e. the determinant of the k × k submatrix determined by taking the elements C ij for i = i 1 , ..i k ; j = j 1 , ..j k . For any two natural numbers b ≥ a we denote the vector ( a, a +1 , ..., b ) by [ a : b ].

texts.orig: The first equality is due to E Q [ V γ n ] = 0. The inequality follows from the LegendreFenchel duality inequality xy ≤ e x + y (log y -1) for all x, y ∈ R by setting x = -V γ n and y = z d Q d P . The last equality is straightforward. From simple calculus it follows that the concave function z → z -z log z -z E Q [ log ( d Q d P )] , z > 0 attains its maximum at z ∗ := exp ( -E Q [ log ( d Q d P )]) and the corresponding maximal value is also z ∗ . This completes the proof of (2.4).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Computational Results
texts.text: 3. Computational Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start this section by providing an explicit algorithm for the computation of ˆ Q and ˆ Γ from (1.2). Recall the notations from Sections 1,2. In view of Corollary 3.2 in [4] and the fact that ˆ Q ∈ S D , we look for a matrix R := ˆ Q -1 that satisfies R ij = Λ ij for i, j < D + 1 and has vanishing super and sub D + 1 minors, i.e. minors of the form R I J where I = ( i 1 , ..., i D +1 ), J = ( j 1 , ..., j D +1 ) that satisfy one of the inequalities j 1 > i D +1 -D (super minors) or i 1 > j D +1 -D (sub minors). The matrix R is symmetric hence we can restrict ourselves to looking only at super minors. Since Λ is positive definite and R ij = Λ ij for i, j < D +1, it follows that the principal minors R [ i +1: i + D ] [ i +1: i + D ] > 0 for all i ≤ n -D -1. Thus, from Theorem 3.4 in [4] we conclude that the matrix R := ˆ Q -1 is the unique symmetric matrix that satisfies R ij = Λ ij for | i -j | ≤ D and
texts.text: We start this section by providing an explicit algorithm for the computation of ˆ Q and ˆ Γ from (1.2). Recall the notations from Sections 1,2. In view of Corollary 3.2 in [4] and the fact that ˆ Q ∈ S D , we look for a matrix R := ˆ Q -1 that satisfies R ij = Λ ij for i, j < D + 1 and has vanishing super and sub D + 1 minors, i.e. minors of the form R I J where I = ( i 1 , ..., i D +1 ), J = ( j 1 , ..., j D +1 ) that satisfy one of the inequalities j 1 > i D +1 -D (super minors) or i 1 > j D +1 -D (sub minors). The matrix R is symmetric hence we can restrict ourselves to looking only at super minors. Since Λ is positive definite and R ij = Λ ij for i, j < D +1, it follows that the principal minors R [ i +1: i + D ] [ i +1: i + D ] > 0 for all i ≤ n -D -1. Thus, from Theorem 3.4 in [4] we conclude that the matrix R := ˆ Q -1 is the unique symmetric matrix that satisfies R ij = Λ ij for | i -j | ≤ D and

For example, the first minor R [1:1+ D ] [2:2+ D ] is determined by ( R ij ) , i = 1 , .., D + 1; j = 2 , .., D + 2 which are all set to the corresponding elements of Λ because | i -j | ≤ D except for R 1 D +2 . Hence, in the Laplace expansion for the ( D +1)-minor
texts.text: Observe that it is possible to order the ( D +1)-minors in (3.1) such that each minor is a function of ( D +1) 2 -1 known elements of R , and one unknown element, hence providing a linear equation for this unknown element. For four integers a ≤ i ≤ b ≤ j , denote by [ a : b ] i → j the vector obtained by omitting the index i from [ a : b ], and adding in the last coordinate the index j , i.e. [ a : b ] i → j = ( a, a +1 , .., i -1 , i +1 , .., b, j ). Hence, in the Laplace expansion for the ( D +1)-minor

texts.label: text
texts.orig: all the D -minors are known, and the unknown term R 12+ D appears only in the first term. It is therefore possible to express R 12+ D uniquely as a linear function of the known ( D +1) 2 -1 R ij values.
texts.text: all the D -minors are known, and the unknown term R 12+ D appears only in the first term. It is therefore possible to express R 12+ D uniquely as a linear function of the known ( D +1) 2 -1 R ij values.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once R 1 D +2 is determined, we can express the next ( D + 1)-minor R [2:2+ D ] [3:3+ D ] in terms of ( D + 1) 2 -1 known R ij elements and the unknown element R 2 D +3 , and determine it in a similar manner. Algorithm 1 describes how to proceed and determine all the unknown elements R ij one by one, hence it provides the entire decomposition of Λ into R + ˆ Γ (recall that R = ˆ Q -1 ).

Algorithm 1 describes how to proceed and determine all the unknown elements R ij one by one, hence it provides the entire decomposition of Λ into R + ˆ Γ (recall that R = ˆ Q -1 ).

texts.text: Algorithm 1 Banded Matrix Decomposition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.1. As matrix inversion can be performed in O ( n 2 . 373 ) floating points operations [20], the total number of floating points operations for Algorithm 1 is O ( n 2 . 373 ) (for matrix inversion), plus O ( n 2 × D 2 . 373 ) for computing the minors (see [1]), hence yielding an overall computational complexity of O ( n 2 ( n 0 . 373 + D 2 . 373 ) ) floating point operations. When Σ has a special structure, specialized faster algorithms can be used. For example, if Σ is a Toeplitz matrix, inversion can be performed in O ( n 2 ) floating point operations using Trench's algorithm [19], hence the overall complexity is reduced to O ( n 2 D 2 . 373 ) .
texts.text: Remark 3.1. As matrix inversion can be performed in O ( n 2 . 373 ) floating points operations [20], the total number of floating points operations for Algorithm 1 is O ( n 2 . 373 ) (for matrix inversion), plus O ( n 2 × D 2 . 373 ) for computing the minors (see [1]), hence yielding an overall computational complexity of O ( n 2 ( n 0 . 373 + D 2 . 373 ) ) floating point operations. When Σ has a special structure, specialized faster algorithms can be used. For example, if Σ is a Toeplitz matrix, inversion can be performed in O ( n 2 ) floating point operations using Trench's algorithm [19], hence the overall complexity is reduced to O ( n 2 D 2 . 373 ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we implemented Algorithm 1 in R code, freely available at github. Using this implementation, we present some numerical results for the simple model in Example 1.5, and for a more realistic model of a discretized fractional Brownian motion. A fractional Brownian motion (fBm) [12] with Hurst parameter H ∈ (0 , 1) is a centered Gaussian process B H t , t ≥ 0 with the covariance function
texts.text: Next, we implemented Algorithm 1 in R code, freely available at github. Using this implementation, we present some numerical results for the simple model in Example 1.5, and for a more realistic model of a discretized fractional Brownian motion. A fractional Brownian motion (fBm) [12] with Hurst parameter H ∈ (0 , 1) is a centered Gaussian process B H t , t ≥ 0 with the covariance function

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (3.2) Cov ( B H s , B H t ) = 1 2 ( t 2 H + s 2 H -| t -s | 2 H ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If H = 1 2 we recover the standard Brownian motion. Models of asset prices based on fBm have long attracted the interest of researchers for their properties of longrange dependence, see for instance, [11, 21]. For some explicit computations for portfolio optimization in the fBm framework see [9, 10].
texts.text: If H = 1 2 we recover the standard Brownian motion. Models of asset prices based on fBm have long attracted the interest of researchers for their properties of longrange dependence, see for instance, [11, 21]. For some explicit computations for portfolio optimization in the fBm framework see [9, 10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider a discretized version of the fBm framework. Namely, we fix H ∈ (0 , 1), a time-resolution ∆ t > 0 and a final time T = n ∆ t for some n ∈ N , and consider the fBm market which is active at times 0 , ∆ t, 2∆ t, . . . , n ∆ t = T . The stock price at time k ∆ t is given by S k = S 0 + B H k ∆ t , k = 0 , 1 , . . . , n . In this case µ = 0 and by (3.2) the covariance matrix is the Toeplitz matrix given by
texts.text: We consider a discretized version of the fBm framework. Namely, we fix H ∈ (0 , 1), a time-resolution ∆ t > 0 and a final time T = n ∆ t for some n ∈ N , and consider the fBm market which is active at times 0 , ∆ t, 2∆ t, . . . , n ∆ t = T . The stock price at time k ∆ t is given by S k = S 0 + B H k ∆ t , k = 0 , 1 , . . . , n . In this case µ = 0 and by (3.2) the covariance matrix is the Toeplitz matrix given by

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Σ ij = Cov ( B H ( i +1)∆ t -B H i ∆ t , B H ( j +1)∆ t -B H j ∆ t ) = ∆ t 2 H 2 ( | i -j -1 | 2 H + | i -j +1 | 2 H -2 | i -j | 2 H ) . (3.3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By following carefully the proof of Proposition 1.6 in [14] we obtain that Σ is positive definite. Moreover, when ∆ t is changed Σ is multiplied by a constant scalar, hence for fixed values of n , the delay D and the H parameter, the value is invariant to ∆ t .
texts.text: By following carefully the proof of Proposition 1.6 in [14] we obtain that Σ is positive definite. Moreover, when ∆ t is changed Σ is multiplied by a constant scalar, hence for fixed values of n , the delay D and the H parameter, the value is invariant to ∆ t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We computed numerically the value as a function of the delay D for both models, for n = 64 and µ = 0. The results, displayed in Fig. 1, show the decrease in the value as D in increasing for both models. We first examined the Kac-Murdock-Szeg¨ o covariance matrix Σ ij := ρ | i -j | from Example 1.5. As expected, for any delay D the value is monotonically increasing with the correlations parameter ρ , hence longer delays can be tolerated with only a negligible drop in the value as ρ increases. For example, when there is no delay the value is significantly reduced compared to the maximum 0 only for ρ < 0 . 3, while for a delay of D = 4 a significant drop in the value is observed as soon as ρ < 0 . 9.
texts.text: We computed numerically the value as a function of the delay D for both models, for n = 64 and µ = 0. The results, displayed in Fig. 1, show the decrease in the value as D in increasing for both models. We first examined the Kac-Murdock-Szeg¨ o covariance matrix Σ ij := ρ | i -j | from Example 1.5. As expected, for any delay D the value is monotonically increasing with the correlations parameter ρ , hence longer delays can be tolerated with only a negligible drop in the value as ρ increases. For example, when there is no delay the value is significantly reduced compared to the maximum 0 only for ρ < 0 . 3, while for a delay of D = 4 a significant drop in the value is observed as soon as ρ < 0 . 9.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we examined the covariance matrix representing the discretized fractional Brownian motion from (3.3). For high H values ( H > 0 . 5), the increments are positively correlated hence the value increases with D towards the maximum 0. For H = 0 . 5 we reduce to the independent Brownian motion case, hence the value drops to -1 regardless of the delay. For low H values ( H < 0 . 5), the increments are negatively correlated hence the value increases, but the increase is non-monotonic in H . The reason is that for very low H values the correlation between the increments decays faster to 0 with their time distance, hence a delay results in almost complete loss of information regarding the current price.
texts.text: Next, we examined the covariance matrix representing the discretized fractional Brownian motion from (3.3). For high H values ( H > 0 . 5), the increments are positively correlated hence the value increases with D towards the maximum 0. For H = 0 . 5 we reduce to the independent Brownian motion case, hence the value drops to -1 regardless of the delay. For low H values ( H < 0 . 5), the increments are negatively correlated hence the value increases, but the increase is non-monotonic in H . The reason is that for very low H values the correlation between the increments decays faster to 0 with their time distance, hence a delay results in almost complete loss of information regarding the current price.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The entire computations for each model at a resolution of 0 . 01 of ρ or H (in total computing the value for 100 parameter values × 8 values of D , i.e. 800 different Σ matrices) took ∼ 200 seconds on a standard PC laptop in our implementation. While the value is invariant to the total time T , changing the discretization parameter n may alter the value. Increasing n can be interpreted as either keeping a fixed time horizon T and decreasing ∆ t , or as fixing ∆ t and increasing the horizon
texts.text: The entire computations for each model at a resolution of 0 . 01 of ρ or H (in total computing the value for 100 parameter values × 8 values of D , i.e. 800 different Σ matrices) took ∼ 200 seconds on a standard PC laptop in our implementation. texts.orig: , this make sense since the continuous time fBm model leads to arbitrage (see for instance [16, 5]). For example, as shown in Figure 1, for H = 0 . 2 and D = 1 the value is roughly -0 . 426. The value increases to -0 . 123 and -0 . 007 for n = 128 and 256, respectively. Increasing the delay D n with n while keeping the ratio D n n fixed amounts to refining the discretization towards the continuous limit with a fixed continuous delay 0 < D < T . The value appears to converge to a negative number for this case (i.e. for D n n = 1 64 ), with -0 . 283 for n = 256 and -0 . 228 for n = 1024. A more thorough theoretical and numeric investigation of the scaling of the value as a function of the problem's parameters for this and other models remains for future work.

The value appears to converge to a negative number for this case (i.e. for D n n = 1 64 ), with -0 . 283 for n = 256 and -0 . 228 for n = 1024. A more thorough theoretical and numeric investigation of the scaling of the value as a function of the problem's parameters for this and other models remains for future work.
